title: Agent Tools and MCP
summary: Learn how agents use tools to take action, and how MCP connects agents to the world.

concepts.name: What are Tools?
concepts.explanation: Tools are functions that an agent can call to do things it cannot do by itself. For example: searching the web, reading a file, checking the weather, or saving data. Without tools, an agent can only talk. With tools, it can act.

concepts.name: How Tool Calling Works
concepts.explanation: When an agent needs information or needs to do something, it calls a tool by name and passes inputs. The tool runs and returns a result. The agent reads the result and continues its work.

concepts.name: What is MCP?
concepts.explanation: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI agents to discover and connect to tools and data sources. Think of MCP like a USB standard — any device that follows USB can plug into any USB port. Any tool that follows MCP can plug into any MCP-compatible agent.

concepts.name: Why MCP Matters
concepts.explanation: Before MCP, every agent needed custom code to connect to each tool. MCP creates one standard way to connect, making agents more reusable and tools more shareable.

quiz_questions.question: What is the main purpose of a tool in an agent system?
options: A) To make the agent look smart
B) To let the agent take real actions like searching or saving data
C) To slow the agent down
D) To replace the agent's memory
quiz_questions.explanation: Tools extend what an agent can do — from just talking to actually acting in the world.

quiz_questions.question: What does MCP stand for?
quiz_questions.explanation: MCP is the Model Context Protocol — a standard for connecting agents to tools and data.

quiz_questions.question: Which real-world object is MCP most similar to?
quiz_questions.explanation: Like USB, MCP is a standard so any compatible tool can plug into any compatible agent.
